title: Issue #1858: agent authorization commands
keyFindings: The requested command shape is `hive auth codex login` and `hive auth claude login`.
The author wants those commands to be callable through Telegram for convenience.
The follow-up requirement explicitly asks for multi-auth support rather than a single global auth.

title: Prepared PR #1859
keyFindings: The prepared PR started as a draft with only a `.gitkeep` scaffold.
No review comments, conversation comments, or reviews were present when the case study was written.

title: docker-git authorization precedent
keyFindings: The referenced project exposes `docker-git auth github login --web`, `docker-git auth codex login --web`, `docker-git auth claude login --web`, and `docker-git auth grok login --web`.
The README describes isolated Docker environments per repository, issue, or pull request.
The project can auto-select an agent based on available authorization.

title: GitHub CLI auth manual
keyFindings: `gh auth` includes login, logout, refresh, setup-git, status, switch, and token commands.
`gh auth switch` selects the active account for a hostname.
`gh auth status` can show active account and authentication state.

title: GitHub CLI environment variables
keyFindings: `GH_TOKEN` and `GITHUB_TOKEN` take precedence for github.com and ghe.com commands.
`GH_CONFIG_DIR` controls where `gh` stores configuration files.
`GH_PROMPT_DISABLED` disables interactive prompting.

title: Claude Code authentication
keyFindings: Claude Code supports Claude.ai subscription credentials, Claude Console/API credentials, cloud provider credentials, and helper-based credentials.
On Linux, subscription credentials live in `~/.claude/.credentials.json` with mode `0600`; `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR` relocates this file on Linux and Windows.
Claude Code chooses credentials by precedence, with cloud provider env vars and `ANTHROPIC_*` env vars ahead of subscription OAuth.

title: Codex authentication
keyFindings: Codex CLI supports ChatGPT sign-in and API key sign-in.
Codex caches credentials in `~/.codex/auth.json` or an OS credential store, controlled by `cli_auth_credentials_store`.
Headless login supports `codex login --device-auth`; copying `auth.json` is documented as a fallback for trusted hosts.

title: Codex environment variables
keyFindings: `CODEX_HOME` sets the root for Codex config, auth, logs, sessions, skills, and package metadata.
`CODEX_API_KEY` supports one non-interactive `codex exec` run.
`CODEX_ACCESS_TOKEN` supports trusted automation and can be piped to `codex login --with-access-token` for persisted login.

title: OpenCode provider authentication
keyFindings: OpenCode uses `/connect` to add provider API keys.
Provider keys added through `/connect` are stored in `~/.local/share/opencode/auth.json`.
OpenCode supports many providers through AI SDK and Models.dev, so auth is provider-specific rather than one global account.

title: Qwen Code authentication
keyFindings: Qwen Code has a standalone `qwen auth` command and an in-session `/auth` command.
`qwen auth status` reports current authentication status.
The docs warn not to commit API keys and to treat terminal output as sensitive if credentials are printed.

title: Gemini CLI authentication
keyFindings: Gemini CLI supports Google login, Gemini API key, and Vertex AI authentication.
Google login caches credentials locally and requires a browser that can communicate with the CLI localhost callback.
Headless mode uses existing cached credentials or environment variables such as `GEMINI_API_KEY`, `GOOGLE_API_KEY`, or `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS`.
